what_learned:
I haven’t set up my own Azure account yet since I’m currently learning through Pluralsight. From what I understand so far, the differences between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS can be explained using a simple real-world analogy along with actual examples.

Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) is like renting an empty apartment. The building and basic utilities are already provided, but you still need to set up everything inside to make it livable. In cloud terms, the provider gives you virtual machines, storage, and networking, but you are responsible for installing and managing software such as web servers (e.g., Nginx) and other dependencies.

A good example of IaaS is Microsoft Azure Virtual Machines or Amazon EC2, where you create, configure, and manage your own virtual servers.

Platform as a Service (PaaS) is like renting a fully prepared apartment where the essential setup (like electricity, water, and even some appliances) is already in place. You don’t need to worry about installing or maintaining the underlying infrastructure, but you can still customize and build what you need. In this model, developers focus on writing and deploying code while the platform handles the environment, runtime, and scaling.

Examples of PaaS include Microsoft Azure App Service and Google App Engine, where you can deploy applications without managing servers.

Software as a Service (SaaS) is like moving into a fully furnished apartment. Everything is ready to use, so you can immediately focus on your tasks without worrying about setup or maintenance. The provider manages both the infrastructure and the application.

Examples of SaaS include Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, and Salesforce, where users simply log in and use the software through a browser.
